passages.explanation: In Caesarea there's a Roman centurion named Cornelius — a Gentile, but a devout, God-fearing man who gives generously to the poor and prays regularly. An angel appears to him in a vision and tells him his prayers and gifts have risen as a memorial before God. The angel instructs him to send men to Joppa to bring back a man named Simon Peter, staying at Simon the tanner's house by the sea. Cornelius obeys immediately, sending two servants and a devout soldier. God is initiating contact with a Gentile household — this is about to change everything.

The next day, as Cornelius' men approach Joppa, Peter goes up on the roof to pray around noon and gets hungry. While food is being prepared, he falls into a trance. He sees heaven opened and a large sheet descending, filled with all kinds of animals — four-footed creatures, reptiles, and birds. A voice says: get up, Peter, kill and eat. Peter refuses: he's never eaten anything impure or unclean. The voice replies: do not call anything impure that God has made clean. This happens three times, then the sheet is taken back up. The vision isn't primarily about food — it's preparing Peter to accept Gentiles as clean.

While Peter is puzzling over the vision, the Spirit tells him three men are looking for him. Go downstairs and go with them without hesitation, because I have sent them. Peter goes down, meets Cornelius' messengers, and hears about the angel's visit. He invites them in to stay the night — already crossing a boundary, since Jews didn't normally host Gentiles. The next day he sets out with them, taking some believers from Joppa as witnesses.

Cornelius has gathered his relatives and close friends — he's expecting Peter. When Peter arrives, Cornelius falls at his feet in reverence. Peter pulls him up: stand up, I'm only a man myself. Peter enters the house and finds a large gathering. He tells them plainly: you know it's against Jewish law for a Jew to associate with or visit a Gentile. But God has shown me that I should not call anyone impure or unclean. So he came without objection. Cornelius explains the angel's visit and says: we're all here in God's presence to listen to everything the Lord has commanded you to tell us. It's a perfect audience.

Peter begins his sermon with a breakthrough statement: I now realise that God does not show favouritism — in every nation, anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him. He then summarises the gospel: God sent his message to Israel through Jesus Christ, who is Lord of all. He recounts Jesus' baptism, ministry of healing, crucifixion, and resurrection. God raised him on the third day and allowed him to be seen — not by everyone, but by chosen witnesses who ate and drank with him after he rose. Jesus commanded them to preach that he's the one God appointed as judge of the living and the dead. Everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name.

While Peter is still speaking, the Holy Spirit falls on all who are listening. The Jewish believers who came with Peter are astonished — the gift of the Holy Spirit has been poured out even on Gentiles. They hear them speaking in tongues and praising God. Peter's response is logical: can anyone withhold water for baptising these people? They've received the Holy Spirit just as we did. He orders them to be baptised in the name of Jesus Christ. This is Pentecost for the Gentiles. The Spirit didn't wait for Peter to finish his sermon or for anyone's permission — God made the decision clear.
